nodes.label: Anthropic's guide to applying multi-day autonomous agentic coding workflows (test oracles, persistent memory, orchestration loops on HPC) to scientific computing tasks, using Claude Opus 4.6 building a differentiable cosmological Boltzmann solver over days as the running example.

New mode of working: instead of a conversational loop managing every step on a tight leash, specify the high-level objective and set a team of agents loose to work autonomously, collapsing projects that would take days/weeks/months into hours.

Long-running autonomous agents fit tasks that are well-scoped with clear success criteria where human oversight can be occasional not continuous (e.g. reimplementing a numerical solver, Fortran-to-modern-language conversion, debugging a codebase against a reference implementation).

The shift to autonomous agent teams is enabled by models getting significantly better at long-horizon tasks over the past year (per METR time-horizons), which is what makes minimal-steering multi-day runs viable.
Anthropic's C compiler project is an existence proof of massive-scale long-running agentic work: Claude worked across ~2,000 sessions to build a C compiler able to compile the Linux kernel.

Task coupling dictates agent topology: embarrassingly-parallel work (C compiler) farms out to many parallel agents, but a deeply-coupled pipeline (Boltzmann solver, where one small numerical error shifts everything downstream) is better suited to a single sequential agent that spawns subagents as needed and bisects discrepancies against a reference.

Debugging deeply-coupled pipelines requires tracing causally through the entire chain and drawing on domain knowledge — a sequential single-agent strength — rather than fan-out, because downstream errors originate far upstream.

Encode the project's deliverables, context, and design decisions in a CLAUDE.md file in the root directory; Claude treats this file specially, keeps it in context, references it for the overall plan, and can edit it as it works to update instructions for future sessions.

In autonomous-team mode, spend most of your time (in consultation with Claude) crafting instructions that clearly articulate deliverables and context, and iterate on the plan locally until it looks reasonable before launching — the plan quality is where human effort now concentrates.

Give the agent a concrete, quantifiable success target it can measure against — here, full feature-parity with reference CLASS plus a 0.1% accuracy target, chosen because 0.1% is the typical agreement between the two canonical codes CLASS and CAMB.

A progress/CHANGELOG.md file is the agent's portable long-term memory across sessions (like lab notes); Claude is instructed in CLAUDE.md to keep tracking progress there so state survives session boundaries.

A good progress file must record failed approaches and WHY they failed (e.g. 'Tried Tsit5 for the perturbation ODE, too stiff, switched to Kvaerno5') — without this, successive sessions re-attempt the same dead ends; also track current status, completed tasks, accuracy tables at checkpoints, and known limitations.

Long-running autonomous scientific work today crucially depends on a test oracle — a reference implementation, a quantifiable objective, or a test suite — that lets the agent know whether it is actually making progress without a human in the loop.

Instruct the agent to expand the test suite and continuously run tests as it works, both to catch bugs and to prevent regressions; here Claude built and ran unit tests against CLASS C source as the reference.

Under-specified test coverage silently caps progress: for a while the agent only tested at a single fiducial parameter point, drastically reducing its bug-catching surface area — the oracle must exercise the whole parameter range, not one point.

Use Git as hands-off coordination and durability: the agent commits and pushes after every meaningful unit of work, giving recoverable history, locally visible progress, and protection against losing work if a compute allocation runs out mid-session.

Encode durability/quality gates as CLAUDE.md rules: 'Commit and push after every meaningful unit of work', 'Run pytest tests/ -x -q before every commit', 'Never commit code that breaks existing passing tests.'

The execution loop: start Claude Code inside a terminal multiplexer (tmux) on a compute node so you can detach, close your laptop, and occasionally check progress remotely (e.g. via GitHub on your phone) while the agent keeps running.

On an HPC cluster, launch the agent via a SLURM batch job (e.g. 48h walltime, GPU allocation) that activates the venv and runs 'tmux new-session -d -s claude "claude; exec bash"', then attach with 'srun --jobid=JOBID --overlap --pty tmux attach'.
Steer a remote long-running agent by SSHing into the cluster to re-prompt or update instructions — and more ergonomically, ask a LOCAL instance of Claude Code to SSH in and run the commands for you.

As models get more capable they need less bespoke orchestration (prompt engineering, RAG, context stuffing), but at any given moment scaffolding still provides a useful capability uplift — orchestration patterns are a temporary crutch that recedes as models improve.

Agentic laziness: on complex multi-part tasks current models sometimes find an excuse to stop before finishing ('It's getting late, let's pick back up tomorrow?'), so an orchestration loop is needed to force completion.

The Ralph loop is a for-loop that kicks the agent back into context whenever it claims completion and asks if it's REALLY done — the agent then admits the task isn't up to spec and keeps working, defeating agentic laziness on long-running tasks.

Bound the completion loop with an explicit success criterion, a max-iterations cap, and a completion-promise token: e.g. '/ralph-loop:ralph-loop "...until 0.1% accuracy across the entire parameter range" --max-iterations 20 --completion-promise "DONE"' iterates up to 20 times until it emits 'DONE'.

Related completion-forcing orchestration patterns include GSD (get-shit-done) and its domain-specific variants (e.g. get-physics-done) and Claude Code's native /loop command; Ralph itself installs via /plugin.

Result: Claude built the differentiable Boltzmann solver from scratch over a few days, reaching sub-percent agreement with the reference CLASS implementation across its various CMB angular power spectrum outputs, with milestones traced over the development trajectory.

The autonomous development trajectory was clunky — the agent made elementary mistakes (tripping over gauge conventions, spending hours on bugs a domain expert would spot instantly) — yet kept making sustained progress toward the stated goal, which is the point of the oracle+loop scaffolding.

A non-domain-expert operator can drive an agent past what expert groups achieved in months-to-years of researcher-time: the author had only high-level familiarity with the cosmology yet steered the agent to feature parity with minimal domain steering.

Anthropic's dynamic workflows in Claude Code let Claude dynamically write orchestration scripts that fan tens-to-hundreds of parallel subagents out over hours-to-days, verifying its own work before returning a single coordinated answer for tasks too big for one pass.

Claude itself dynamically WRITES the orchestration scripts at runtime based on the prompt — the workflow structure is generated, not a fixed pre-authored pipeline, letting one session spin up tens-to-hundreds of parallel subagents.

Some problems (bug hunt across an entire service, migration touching hundreds of files, a plan stress-tested from every angle) are too big for a single agent in one pass, especially in complex legacy codebases — this is the class dynamic workflows target.
Execution model: on kickoff Claude plans dynamically from the prompt, breaks the task into subtasks, and fans the work out across subagents running in parallel, returning one coordinated answer.
Every subagent result is independently checked/verified BEFORE it is folded into the final answer, so the report surfaces only real issues rather than unverified findings.

Convergence loop: agents attack the problem from independent angles while other adversarial agents try to refute what they found, and the run keeps iterating until answers converge — how a workflow reaches results a single pass cannot.

For audits, Claude searches a repo in parallel then runs INDEPENDENT verification on every individual finding — per-finding verification, not a single global review pass — reducing false positives in the surfaced report.
Dynamic workflows are built for parallel and long-running work extending into hours and days, doing complex engineering that previously would have taken weeks.
Durability: progress is saved as the run proceeds, so an interrupted job picks up where it left off instead of restarting — checkpointing makes long unattended runs recoverable.

Coordination happens OUTSIDE the conversation, so the plan stays on track no matter how big the task gets — decoupling orchestration state from the chat context prevents the plan from degrading as the task scales.
Turning on auto mode is recommended for the best dynamic-workflow experience — minimizing interactive interruptions so long unattended runs don't stall waiting on the human.
The Claude Code-specific `ultracode` setting (in the effort menu) sets effort to xhigh while letting Claude decide automatically when to spin up a workflow for a task.
Two ways to start a workflow: ask Claude directly ("Create a workflow"), or switch on `ultracode` so Claude auto-decides — an explicit-invocation vs automatic-triggering choice.

Dynamic workflows consume substantially/meaningfully more tokens than a typical Claude Code session; Anthropic recommends starting on a scoped task to gauge usage — cost is the primary trade-off of massive fan-out.
The first time a workflow triggers, Claude Code shows what's about to run and asks for confirmation — a one-time human gate before committing to a high-cost run.
Organization admins can disable workflows via managed settings, and availability is plan-gated (on by default for Max/Team/Enterprise/API; Pro enables via /config).
Dynamic workflows fill the gap between firing off a single subagent and building out a full agent team — a middle tier of orchestration that supports trusted longer runs without losing visibility.

Use case: codebase-wide bug hunts, profiler-guided optimization audits, and security/hardening audits (auth checks, input validation, unsafe patterns) run in parallel with independent verification per finding.
Use case: large migrations and modernization — framework swaps, API deprecations, and language ports spanning thousands of files, handled end-to-end.

Use case: critical work needing double-checking — the workflow gives Claude independent attempts plus adversarial agents working to break the result before the human sees it, for when a wrong answer is costly.
Proof point: the Bun rewrite ported Zig→Rust with 99.8% of the existing test suite passing, ~750,000 lines of Rust, in eleven days from first commit to merge, done entirely by dynamic workflows.

Bun port used STAGED workflows: workflow 1 mapped the right Rust lifetime for every Zig struct field; workflow 2 wrote every .rs file as a behavior-identical port (hundreds of agents, two reviewers per file); a fix loop then drove build+tests until clean — decomposing a huge port into sequential specialized workflow stages.
Reviewer fan-out pattern: hundreds of agents worked in parallel with TWO independent reviewers assigned to each ported file — per-unit review redundancy to catch errors.
A fix loop drove the build and test suite until both ran clean — an automated verify-and-repair iteration that keeps running until a hard success signal (green build + tests) is reached.

After the port landed, an overnight workflow addressed unnecessary data copies and opened a separate PR for each fix for final human review — unattended optimization pass that packages each change as an independently reviewable PR.
Value claim: work you'd normally plan in quarters now finishes in days; complex engineering that took weeks compresses to days/hours via massive parallelism.

Dynamic workflows let Claude Code write and orchestrate its OWN multi-agent harness on the fly — a custom JavaScript harness built per-task that spawns and coordinates subagents — to solve long-running, massively parallel, structured, or adversarial tasks the single-context default harness fails at.

A dynamic workflow is a harness Claude writes on the fly, custom-built for the task at hand, built on top of Claude Code — Claude authors and executes its own orchestration code rather than running inside the fixed default harness.
nodes.source_location: section: intro / How dynamic workflows work

nodes.label: Dynamic workflows execute a JavaScript file exposing a few special functions that spawn and coordinate subagents, plus standard JS (JSON, Math, Array) for data processing — so orchestration logic is real code (loops, branching, aggregation), not prose.

The default Claude Code harness is built for coding but generalizes because many tasks resemble coding tasks; peak-performance non-coding tasks (Research, security analysis, agent teams, Code Review) historically needed custom hand-built harnesses on top of Claude Code.
Workflows can be shared and reused with others — an authored harness is a portable artifact, not a one-off, so proven orchestration patterns become reusable building blocks.

Dynamic workflows often use MORE tokens and are best suited for complex, high-value tasks — the orchestration overhead only pays off when the task is hard enough to justify it; don't reach for a workflow on simple work.

A workflow can decide WHICH model each agent uses, letting Claude choose the intelligence level needed per subtask — cheap/fast models for simple steps, stronger models for hard ones (model/effort routing).

A workflow can decide whether each subagent runs in its own git worktree, giving Claude control over the isolation level needed — parallel agents can work without stepping on each other's filesystem state.

Durability: if a workflow is interrupted (user action, quitting the terminal), resuming the session lets the workflow pick up where it left off — checkpointed orchestration state survives interruption, essential for long/24h+ unattended runs.

The default harness must both PLAN and EXECUTE in the same context window; this works for many coding tasks but breaks down on long-running, massively parallel, highly structured, and/or adversarial tasks — the core reason to move orchestration into a separate workflow layer.

Agentic laziness: on a complex multi-part task Claude stops early and declares the job done after only partial progress (e.g. addressing 35 of 50 security-review items) — a failure mode that grows the longer it works in one context window.

Self-preferential bias: Claude tends to prefer its own results/findings, especially when asked to verify or judge them against a rubric — so the generator must not also be the verifier; use a separate agent to judge.

Goal drift: gradual loss of fidelity to the original objective across many turns, worsened by compaction — each summarization step is lossy and drops details like edge-case requirements or 'don't do X' constraints.

A workflow combats laziness, self-bias, and goal drift by orchestrating separate Claude subagents each with their OWN context window and a focused, isolated goal — fresh bounded context per subtask prevents the single-context degradation modes.

Verification-loop pattern (from example prompts): form competing theories about a bug/race and 'don't stop until one theory survives the evidence', and double-check top results — an explicit adversarial/verification loop that keeps running until evidence resolves it.

Adversarial multi-perspective fan-out (from examples): different agents attack the same artifact from opposing viewpoints (investor/customer/competitor tearing apart a business plan) — parallel subagents with distinct adversarial mandates surface what a single pass misses.

Brainstorm-then-tournament pattern (from examples): a workflow generates many candidate options then runs a tournament to pick the top few (naming a CLI tool) — fan-out to generate, structured elimination to converge.

Human-in-the-loop via AskUserQuestion: a workflow can interview the user (e.g. to build a ranking rubric) using AskUserQuestion — structured human input is a first-class step, letting otherwise-autonomous runs pull in judgment when needed.

Self-improvement via session mining (from examples): a workflow goes through the last N sessions, mines recurring corrections the user keeps making, and turns them into CLAUDE.md rules — the agent improves its own future behavior from its own history.

Dynamic vs static workflows: a static workflow is coded ahead of time with the Claude Agent SDK or 'claude -p' to coordinate multiple Claude instances; a dynamic workflow is authored by Claude at runtime for the specific task — the distinction is who writes the harness and when.

Anthropic's Claude Code team frames 'loop engineering' as designing agents that repeat cycles of work until a stop condition is met, progressing from turn-based to goal-based, time-based, and proactive loops — each with distinct triggers, stop criteria, primitives, task fit, and token-management levers.
Core definition: a loop is an agent repeating cycles of work UNTIL a stop condition is met — the stop condition, not the trigger, is what makes iteration bounded and safe rather than open-ended.

Loops are classified along four axes — how they are TRIGGERED, how they are STOPPED, which Claude Code PRIMITIVE is used, and which TASK TYPE is most appropriate — giving a decision framework for picking a loop shape.
Design principle: not all tasks need complex loops — start with the simplest solution and adopt richer loop patterns selectively, only when a single turn or manual direction proves insufficient.

Turn-based loop: triggered by a user prompt, stops when Claude judges the task done or needs more context; best for short one-off tasks not part of a schedule; usage managed by writing specific prompts and stronger verification to reduce turn count.
The 'agentic loop' is the atomic per-turn cycle: Claude gathers context, takes action, checks its own work, repeats if needed, then responds — the human directs each turn by writing the next prompt.

Encode your manual verification steps as a SKILL.md so Claude can self-check its work end-to-end instead of relying on the human to inspect each turn — the key lever for reducing turns and enabling longer unattended runs.

Verification should give Claude tools/connectors to SEE, MEASURE, or INTERACT with the result; the more quantitative the checks, the easier it is for Claude to self-verify reliably (e.g. browser screenshots, zero console errors, performance traces / Core Web Vitals).

Goal-based loop (/goal): triggered by a real-time prompt, stops when the goal is achieved OR a max turn count is reached; best for tasks with verifiable exit criteria; usage managed by explicit completion criteria plus turn caps like 'stop after 5 tries'.

In /goal, each time Claude tries to stop an EVALUATOR MODEL checks the user-defined success condition and sends Claude back to work until the goal is met or the turn cap hits — externalizing the 'good enough' judgment so the agent can't end the loop prematurely.

Deterministic exit criteria (number of tests passed, a Lighthouse/score threshold) are especially effective for goal-based loops because they remove ambiguity about completion — the agent iterates against an objective target, not a subjective sense of done.

Explicit turn caps ('stop after 5 tries') bound cost and prevent runaway iteration when a goal is unreachable — a safety valve pairing with the success-criteria stop condition for unattended goal loops.

Time-based loop (/loop and /schedule): triggered by a time interval, stops when the user cancels or the work completes (PR merges, queue empties); best for recurring work or interfacing with external systems; usage managed via longer intervals or event-based reaction.
Time-based loops fit work where the task stays constant and only the inputs change (e.g. summarizing Slack messages every morning) — the loop re-runs a fixed procedure over fresh data.

A simple way to interface with an external system is to poll it on an interval and react to what changed (e.g. a PR that may receive reviews or fail CI) — turning outside state changes into loop iterations.

Manage time-based loop cost by preferring event-driven reaction over fixed intervals (or lengthening the interval) — reacting to events rather than polling on a timer minimizes wasted token spend on unchanged state.

Each loop type carries an explicit token/usage-management lever — specific prompts + verification (turn-based), completion criteria + turn caps (goal-based), longer intervals + event reaction (time-based) — so maintaining code quality and controlling cost are co-designed with the loop, not afterthoughts.

Turn the manual checks you already run by hand into Claude Code skills so the agent closes its own verification feedback loop — checking and fixing its work iteratively — letting you work on something else while it iterates unattended.

The core agentic coding loop has three phases: (1) gather context, (2) take action, (3) verify results — and if verification fails it loops back to gather more context, forming an iterative self-correcting cycle.

Verification is how an agent checks its own work BEFORE responding; a verification loop is an iterative process where Claude checks and attempts to fix the work rather than returning unvalidated output — the mechanism that makes autonomous iteration trustworthy.

Claude already verifies for free by observing deterministic signals in the codebase — type checkers, linters, tests, and runtime errors; whatever it cannot infer from these becomes the manual steps a human takes, which are the candidates for encoding as loops.

When you find yourself making the same small corrections every time Claude implements a feature, encode those repeated manual checks as a verification loop so the agent enforces them itself — this is what lets a run proceed unattended while you do other work.
Built-in /verify skill: builds, runs, and observes the changes in your application — a ready-made verification loop for exercising the app end-to-end.

Toolchain verification: Claude catches and acts on error codes and warnings from any tool you provide (e.g. a linter); best practice is to list the exact build and test commands in CLAUDE.md so Claude doesn't have to infer them.

Code Review (research preview) is a managed multi-agent service running an automated review pass on PRs in enabled repos; you close the loop by commenting @claude on a finding (given GitHub Actions setup) so the fix is applied automatically.
GitHub Actions verification: define a job that invokes Claude with a verification skill so the same checks you run locally fire automatically on every push or PR — moving the loop into CI.
Spec validation skill verifies each change against a markdown spec kept in the repo and looks to fix violations — grounding verification in a written specification rather than only code signals.

Rubrics in Claude Managed Agents (beta): a separate grader agent verifies outcomes against a rubric, and failures loop back for rework automatically — a two-agent generate-then-grade pattern with automatic self-correction directly relevant to orchestrated unattended runs.

To author a loop, first write down everything you do every time — the best-practices version in plain English, as you'd hand it to a new teammate on day one — turning tacit manual review into an explicit reusable check.

If you can't articulate a check, ask Claude for best practices first and edit from there; your version differs on a few specific points, and those differences are exactly the project-specific knowledge worth capturing.

A verification check need not be qualitative: project-specific deterministic rules no generic linter catches (e.g. 'reject any migration that drops a column without a backfill step') qualify for capture — anything you keep enforcing by hand belongs in a loop.

The most common way to encode a repetitive check into a verification loop is to write it as a skill; the fastest route is the skill-creator plugin, which lets Claude interview you about your workflow to generate the skill.

You can hand-write a verification skill by dropping a markdown SKILL.md in .claude/skills/ inside the project — the simplest one is a few lines of frontmatter (name, description, allowed-tools) plus a plain-English body of instructions.

A skill's allowed-tools frontmatter (e.g. [Read, Edit, Grep]) scopes it to the minimal tool set it needs — narrowing capability per-skill, useful for reducing risk and permission surface in unattended verification runs.

Canonical verification-skill pattern (verify-log-hygiene example): read the relevant paths in the current diff, report each violation with file:line, then fix it in place — inspect, enumerate, remediate within one loop.

Anthropic runs large-scale code migrations (e.g. Bun's million-line Zig-to-Rust port) with Claude Code by treating them as agent-driven parallel workstreams governed by verification loops, phase gates, and multi-model routing — the core insight being you don't fix the code, you fix the process/loop that produced it.

Core migration principle: you don't fix the code, you fix the process (loop) that produced the code — debugging and improving the generating process yields correct output at scale rather than patching individual outputs.

Migrations were driven by 'dynamic workflows' in Claude Code combined with Claude Fable 5 and Opus 4.8, enabling individual developers to port tens-to-hundreds-of-thousands-of-line packages that were previously multi-year efforts.

Bun Zig-to-Rust port: ~1 million lines produced in under two weeks with 100% of Bun's existing test suite passing in CI before merge; 19 regressions surfaced post-merge and were all fixed — a real proof point for AI-driven multi-week autonomous migration.

Mike Krieger's Python-to-TypeScript migration (165,000 lines over a weekend) used hundreds of agents, eight phase gates, three adversarial review rounds, and a final parity check diffing every command's output against the Python original.

Phase gates structure a long migration into sequential checkpoints (Krieger used eight) that each workstream must pass before advancing, giving a durable, resumable staging of otherwise unbounded autonomous work.
Adversarial review rounds (three in Krieger's port) subject generated code to critique passes so defects are caught by opposing agents before the migration is trusted.
A final parity check diffs every command's output between the migrated and original codebase, providing an objective end-state verification that the port is behaviorally equivalent.

AI lowers migration risk because the worst-case outcome is now 'delete the branch and try again' rather than maintaining two parallel codebases for years at 90% parity — cheap failure changes the risk calculus for long-deferred projects.

A migration still needs a justifiable business case: million-line ports no longer cost $3-4M over four years but still cost tens-to-hundreds of thousands of dollars, so token/compute cost must be weighed against the payoff.

Concrete cost anchor: the Bun migration consumed 5.9 billion uncached input tokens and 690 million output tokens (~$165,000 at API pricing); Krieger's main port was 27 million tokens — real token budgets for planning unattended migration runs.

Migration payoff example: Krieger's port cut a Python single-binary compile from ~8 min/platform (~30 min across the build matrix) to ~2 seconds, made the binary start 6x faster, and retired a separate deployment pipeline.

Fable 5 and Opus 4.8 are particularly good at delegating, directing, and verifying parallel workstreams with subagents while finding multiple paths toward a stated goal — the capability that makes agent-driven migration viable.

Migrations parallelize well because the work splits into thousands of independent units (files, crates) that agents process simultaneously instead of one waiting on another — the structural basis for subagent fan-out.
The old codebase serves as a comprehensive spec and reference for the model, and becomes the source for building the translation guide that translation agents follow.

A built-in referee (the existing test suite) lets agents verify their own work against an objective ground truth, so the model can grind for days without a human arbitrating quality — objective verification is what enables truly unattended long runs.

'The queue writes itself': every failed compiler or test run automatically becomes the next work item for an agent, so failures generate the work backlog rather than stalling the run — a self-feeding verification loop.

Consistency mechanism: reviewers cite the specific rule behind every finding so a violation becomes a queue item instead of a quiet divergence, and when an agent hits an edge case the fix becomes a rule every subsequent agent follows — drift has nowhere to hide.

Advisory pattern: multiple model classes are combined (Fable for key advisory/directing steps, cheaper classes for bulk execution) to optimize token consumption across a large migration — explicit model/effort routing for cost control.

Claude Fable 5 field guide: work quality is bottlenecked by the human's ability to clarify the model's 'unknowns' (the gap between the map you give it and the territory it works in), so working with Fable is an iterative process of discovering and resolving unknowns before, during, and after implementation.

Map vs territory framing: the 'map' is what you give Claude (prompts, skills, context — a representation of the work); the 'territory' is where the work actually happens (the codebase, the real world, its constraints). The unknowns are precisely the gap between map and territory.

An 'unknown' is any point where Claude hits something not covered by its instructions and must act on its best guess of what you want; the more work being done in one run, the more unknowns the agent is likely to encounter and mis-guess.

Rationale: Fable is the first model where the bottleneck on output quality is not the model's capability but the human's ability to surface and clarify its unknowns — so investment should shift from prompting harder to reducing ambiguity.
nodes.source_location: blog body: 'bottlenecked by my ability to clarify its unknowns'

nodes.label: Rationale: planning ahead is not sufficient — unknowns surface deep in implementation, and a discovered unknown can reveal that the whole problem should be solved a different way; you cannot enumerate all unknowns up front.
nodes.source_location: blog body: 'just planning ahead isn't always enough'

nodes.label: Core workflow: discovering unknowns is iterative and spans three phases — pre-implementation, during implementation, and post-implementation — each with its own techniques, rather than a single up-front planning step.

Four-quadrant decomposition of a problem: Known Knowns (what's in your prompt), Known Unknowns (gaps you're aware of), Unknown Knowns (things too obvious to write down but you'd recognize when seen), and Unknown Unknowns (things you haven't considered at all / don't know how good is possible).

Rationale: the best agentic coders have relatively few unknowns AND deliberately assume unknowns exist; reducing and planning for unknowns IS the core skill of agentic coding, and it is a learnable skill that improves by working with Claude.

Rationale (over- vs under-specification trade-off): too specific and Claude follows instructions even when a pivot would be better; too vague and Claude fills gaps with generic industry best practices that may not fit — failing to account for unknowns makes you fail both ways at once.

Technique: use Claude itself as the unknown-discovery engine — it searches the codebase and the internet extremely fast, knows more than you about the average topic, and iterates from failure quickly, so it can surface your unknowns faster than you can alone.
nodes.source_location: blog body: 'Claude can help you discover your unknowns faster'

nodes.label: Technique: the most important input is context about your starting point — tell Claude where you are in your thinking, disclose your experience with the problem and codebase, and let it act as a thought partner, so it can calibrate how to collaborate.
nodes.source_location: blog body: 'give Claude context about your starting point'

nodes.label: Catalog of unknown-uncovering patterns by phase: Pre-implementation (blind spot pass, brainstorms & prototype, interviews, references, implementation plan); During implementation (implementation notes); Post-implementation (pitches & explainers, quizzes).
nodes.source_location: blog body: 'In this article I detail some of the patterns'

nodes.label: Blind-spot-pass pattern: when entering unfamiliar code or work you have many unknown unknowns; ask Claude explicitly (using the literal words 'blind spot pass' and 'unknown unknowns') to find and explain your blind spots, always giving it context on who you are and what you already know so it can start collaborating well.
nodes.source_location: blog body: Blind Spot Pass section

nodes.label: Brainstorms-and-prototypes pattern: when an area is full of unknown knowns (criteria you can only define once you see them), ask Claude to brainstorm and build prototypes so you can recognize what you want by seeing candidate options rather than specifying them up front.
nodes.source_location: blog body: Brainstorms and prototypes section (truncated)

nodes.label: Dynamic workflows let Claude write its own orchestration program on the fly and run it across many separate Claudes — each with a clean context window and one focused job — to defeat the three failure modes (laziness, self-bias, goal drift) that a single long-running context window hits.
A 'harness' is the program that wraps the model: it decides what Claude reads, when it acts, and how its output gets checked. Default Claude Code is one fixed harness built for coding.
A 'workflow' (dynamic workflow) is a harness that Claude writes itself, in real time, tailored to the specific task — swapping in a custom harness in place of the default coding one.

Agentic laziness: on a complex multi-part task Claude stops before finishing and declares done after partial progress (reviews 35 of 50 files) — not lying, but its working memory filled, it lost track of what's left, and rationalized a stopping point.

Self-preferential bias: when Claude verifies/judges its own results against a rubric it prefers its own findings — a grader that wrote the answer is biased — and it worsens when verification happens inside the same conversation that produced the work.

Goal drift: across many turns fidelity to the original objective erodes, mainly via compaction (lossy summarization when a conversation outgrows context) which compresses away exactly the edge-case requirements and 'don't do X' constraints.
All three failure modes get worse the longer a single context window runs and the more jobs are piled into it — so the fix follows directly from the diagnosis.

The fix: instead of one Claude carrying everything, orchestrate many Claudes, each with its own fresh context window and a single focused goal — structurally removing (not tweaking away) the conditions that cause each failure mode.

Isolation defeats laziness because no single agent holds the whole 50-item task — one agent reviews one file and can't tire of a list it never saw — while the orchestrator tracks coverage so nothing silently falls off the end.

Isolation defeats self-preferential bias because the agent that produces a result is never the agent that judges it: a separate verifier in a separate context window, given only the rubric and the output, has no prior work to defend and grades on the merits.

Isolation defeats goal drift because each agent's window is short — it starts with a crisp goal, does one job, and returns before compaction kicks in; the original objective lives in the orchestrator's fresh instructions to each agent, not buried under 40 turns of summarization.
The entire thesis: spread the work across isolated agents and check the results before they reach you — every later pattern is just a different shape of that.
A dynamic workflow is not a magic mode — it is a real JavaScript file Claude writes on the spot and then executes, making the feature concrete and inspectable.
Every workflow file starts with a `meta` metadata declaration — a plain literal (no variables or function calls) giving name, description, and phases (e.g. ['review','verify']).
`agent(prompt, opts)` spawns one subagent and returns its final text (or a validated object if given a schema) — the unit of isolated work: one Claude, one clean context window, one goal.

`pipeline(items, stage1, stage2, ...)` runs each item through every stage independently with NO barrier between stages (item A can be in stage 3 while item B is in stage 1) — the right default because a file's verification can start the moment that file's review finishes.

`parallel(thunks)` runs a set of tasks at once and waits for ALL to complete before moving on — it is a barrier, used only when the next step genuinely needs every prior result at once (e.g. deduplicating across the full set or comparing items against each other).
Handing `agent()` a structured-output JSON Schema forces the subagent to return a validated object and retries on mismatch, so downstream stages get clean data instead of prose to parse.
A token budget caps how much the whole run can spend — necessary because workflows are not cheap.
`isolation: 'worktree'` gives an agent its own copy of the repository, needed when several agents edit files in parallel and must not clobber each other.
Concurrency is capped automatically and there is a hard ceiling on how many agents a single run can spawn, so a workflow cannot quietly fork-bomb the machine.

A hand-built static harness (Agent SDK / `claude -p`) must handle every edge case for every task so it ends up generic; a dynamic workflow is the opposite — Opus 4.8 is smart enough to write a correct harness on demand, tailored exactly to the one task in front of it, with no edge cases to pre-solve.

Triggering a workflow: ask Claude to 'make a workflow', or enable the `ultracode` effort setting (sends xhigh effort and lets Claude decide when a task warrants a custom harness) — and pair either with Auto Mode so a run spawning dozens of subagents doesn't stop on every permission prompt.

Six composable patterns cover nearly every workflow, each a different arrangement of 'spawn isolated agents, then combine their work'; prompting Claude with the right pattern by name gives the sharpest results.

Classify-and-act pattern: a classifier agent decides what kind of task/item it is, then routes to specialist agents (e.g. support tickets → bug/billing/feature specialists); the classifier can also run at the end to label or package final output.
Fan-out-and-synthesize pattern: split a task into many small steps, run one agent on each, then synthesize the results (the synthesize step being a barrier). [source truncated mid-sentence]

To run Claude Code / Codex coding agents unattended for 24h+, build a harness that minimizes interruptions: grant all permissions (in a sandbox), make agents self-verify against explicit done-criteria, add agentic code review, and run agents remotely so they survive closing your laptop — because human review, not implementation, is now the bottleneck.

The key to running many coding agents in parallel is long-running sessions that are interrupted as little as possible (few stops for human input, permissions, etc.); uninterrupted duration is what lets one human supervise many concurrent agents.

Since Claude Opus 4.5, the coding bottleneck shifted from writing code to human REVIEW time — checking correctness and spec-conformance is what's slow, so the payoff is minimizing human review by having agents implement AND review before handoff.

Frontier coding agents (notably the latest Claude Fable model and GPT-5.6) are increasingly 'eager' to complete assigned goals and can run autonomously for longer without stalling — model capability is a precondition for long runs, but insufficient alone.

The model alone doesn't solve long-running autonomy; you also need a harness and structure around the coding workflow (environment, permissions, self-verification) so agents can run for extended periods — capability + scaffolding together.
nodes.source_location: intro 'the agents in themselves don't solve everything'

nodes.label: Pre-grant agents all the permissions they need (Codex/Claude Code auto-mode or skip-all-permissions settings) so they never stall asking for approval — the permission prompts are almost always accepted anyway, so waiting on them is pure wasted time.
Run agents in sandbox mode so they cannot delete or damage anything outside their own workspace — the safety mechanism that makes skipping permission prompts acceptable.

Sandboxing is what makes it SAFE to grant all permissions: OpenAI admitted the latest GPT model deleted users' files under rare specific circumstances, so a sandbox (plus backups) contains the blast radius of an over-permissioned autonomous agent.

Back everything up as defense-in-depth for autonomous agents: keep all code in GitHub (secure storage + versioning) and run machine backups (e.g. Time Machine on Mac), so a destructive agent action is recoverable.

Make agents verify their own work by writing task descriptions that state not just WHAT to achieve but explicitly HOW the agent knows it's done — usually by telling it how to test the implementation; verifiability is a property of the prompt + the tools you give.

The most important part of a task spec is telling the agent how to KNOW it has completed the work (a testable done-condition); without an explicit success criterion an unattended agent can't decide when to stop or self-correct.

Concrete self-verification pattern: for a UI/design task, give the agent browser (Chrome) access and instruct it to open both the target design and its own implementation, take screenshots of each, compare them, and iterate until equal.

Instruct the self-verifying agent to surface discrepancies it cannot reconcile (e.g. a design that's technically infeasible in the codebase) back to the human for a decision, rather than silently guessing — bounded escalation keeps autonomy safe.

The verify-your-own-work philosophy generalizes to nearly all programming tasks: describe the task in detail, say what to watch for, and specify a verification method (screenshots, running a test suite, etc.) suited to that task.

Add an agentic code-review step so an agent reviews the agent-written code, largely eliminating the need for human review except on very critical parts — the author argues modern agents review well enough to catch nearly all bugs from newly pushed code.

Agentic code review is far EASIER to adopt than in-task self-verification: self-verification needs careful task-specific prompting and tool access, whereas a review agent is a generic drop-in step over a diff — lower setup cost for a large review-time saving.

The author rates Codex as the best code-review agent; two ways to run it: (1) Codex's GitHub integration — tag Codex on a PR and it auto-reviews; (2) the CLI — point it at two branches to diff-review one against another.

Prefer the Codex CLI branch-diff review over the GitHub PR-tag review: the CLI is faster and avoids the delay that often occurs when Codex is tagged on a GitHub PR — latency matters for keeping an unattended loop moving.

For truly long runs, run agents remotely (cloud or an always-on second machine) so they aren't interrupted when you close your laptop — remote execution decouples agent uptime from your personal device's availability.

Local vs remote agent execution is a trade-off: local is simpler and gives full visibility into what the agent (and its browser) is doing, but risks interruption when you close/sleep the machine; remote never gets interrupted but offers less overview and is clunkier to set up.
Disabling laptop sleep-on-lid-close to keep local agents alive is a poor workaround — in the author's experience it makes the machine run very hot; use a remote/always-on host instead.

Practical setup: a primary travel machine for most agents plus a secondary always-on machine hosting persistent bots (OpenClaw) and spun-up Claude Code/Codex sessions that run indefinitely without interruption.

Running agents in the cloud is an option but carries downsides the author avoids: you must store documents/secrets in the cloud (a security risk) and setup is clunky — a self-hosted always-on machine sidesteps the secret-exposure concern.

Claude Code team member Thariq Shihipar shares how to run agents for long periods via /loop, /goal, and workflows, why Anthropic cut Claude Code's system prompt by 80% (smarter models need less direction), and how he coordinates multiple Claude Code agents through Slack.

Claude Code ships three distinct long-run primitives — /loop, /goal, and workflows — that each get the agent to run autonomously for extended periods, but they work differently from one another (different mechanisms for planning, iterating, and self-continuing).

Anthropic cut Claude Code's system prompt by 80%: as models get smarter they need less direction, fewer constraints, and fewer examples — the guidance that helped weaker models now over-constrains stronger ones.

In-context examples actively constrain a strong model — it reads them as 'you want things like this example'; removing the examples lets the model respond more free-form and use its own judgment, so fewer examples can improve behavior.

Trim your context/prompt when a new model is released — the latest models 'need more room to run', so prompt engineering should be re-evaluated and reduced with each model upgrade rather than carried forward unchanged.

Thariq uses Slack as a coordination layer to run and orchestrate multiple Claude Code agents in parallel — a chat channel becomes the shared interface for dispatching and tracking several concurrent agents.
Planning is an iterative process of exploring, investigating, and discovering what you don't yet know — not a one-shot upfront artifact; the plan is refined as the agent surfaces unknowns.

A common failure mode is humans glazing over the AI's generated plans — you must actually read the plan before letting the agent execute it, because unreviewed plans propagate errors through long autonomous runs.
Use HTML artifacts as a medium for the agent to plan and to learn — rendering plans/knowledge as inspectable HTML gives the human a concrete surface to review and steer the long-running work.

Anthropic's five Claude Code agentic workflow patterns — sequential, operator/orchestrator, split-and-merge, agent teams, and headless — each fit a different task complexity and human-oversight level, letting Claude plan/execute/verify/iterate on multi-step work beyond a single stateless prompt.

A single LLM call is stateless — it takes input, produces output, and stops — so it cannot handle multi-step engineering work like schema migration + code updates, run-fix-rerun test loops, or coordinating review/docs/deploy in sequence; agentic workflows exist to chain capability across steps where each step builds on the last.

Claude can use tools (file reads, shell commands, API calls, web searches) and can spawn or coordinate with other agents when a task is too large or complex for one context window — the mechanism underlying every multi-agent pattern.

Pattern selection principle: the right workflow depends on the task's complexity and how much human oversight is needed — the five patterns map directly onto Anthropic's 'building effective agents' guidance; don't reach for multi-agent orchestration when a simpler pattern suffices.

Sequential workflow: Claude executes ordered steps as a pipeline where each step's output feeds the next (e.g. read spec → generate impl → write tests → run tests) — no branching, no parallelism, no delegation.

Use sequential when the task has a clear predictable order, each step's output is the direct input to the next, the whole task fits in one or two context windows, and you want maximum predictability + easy debugging.

Sequential trades throughput for simplicity: easy to reason about, debug, and restart mid-way if it fails — but running N independent tasks serially wastes time when there's no real dependency, and a deep pipeline can exhaust a single context window if intermediate results are large.

Sequential setup in Claude Code: drive it with a detailed initial prompt that breaks the task into explicit phases, or a script that calls Claude repeatedly passing each step's results forward; use CLAUDE.md to set persistent context that carries across steps in a session.

Operator (orchestrator) pattern: one controlling 'brain' Claude instance receives the high-level goal, decomposes it into subtasks, delegates each to specialized subagents or tool calls, then synthesizes results — a manager-worker structure (e.g. operator directs subagents scanning auth / DB queries / input-validation for a security audit).

Use the operator pattern when the task exceeds a single context window, different parts need different expertise/focus, you want a clear separation of planning from execution, and you need centralized control over how subtasks are prioritized and assembled.

The operator scales because it doesn't need to know how each subagent does its work — it only interprets results and makes decisions, keeping the coordinator's reasoning independent of worker internals.

The operator itself is the risk: if its context fills with too many subagent results, quality degrades — so you must design clear operator↔subagent interfaces (vague handoffs produce vague results) and give the orchestrator explicit guidance on task decomposition, subagent-failure handling, and synthesizing divergent outputs.

Operator setup in Claude Code: use the --allowedTools flag to control what the orchestrating instance can delegate; the operator spawns subagent instances via shell or API calls, collects results, and continues, with CLAUDE.md files giving each subagent role-specific context.

Split-and-merge (parallel) pattern: a coordinator splits the task into independent no-dependency chunks, multiple Claude instances run simultaneously, then results are merged into a final output (e.g. document 50 functions as 10 parallel batches of 5, merge the docstrings).

Use split-and-merge when tasks partition cleanly with no cross-dependencies, wall-clock speed matters, subtasks are self-contained enough to run in isolation, and you're processing large volumes of similar items (files, records, text chunks).

Split-and-merge's payoff is near-linear wall-clock speedup — ten parallel workers finish ~10x the work in the same time — which matters most for large-scale code analysis, bulk refactoring, and processing pipelines.

Split-and-merge's hard part is the merge: parallel results with inconsistent output formats or conflicts need careful reconciliation, and you must handle partial failures (what happens when 3 of 10 instances fail) rather than assuming all branches succeed.

Cost scales with fan-out: running ten instances in parallel costs roughly ten times a single sequential instance, so for high-volume workflows the token/compute bill adds up quickly and must be weighed against the speed gain.
Defining a consistent output schema for each parallel branch makes merging significantly easier — a shared contract across fan-out workers is the concrete lever that tames the merge step.

Agent teams extend the operator pattern into a persistent cross-functional team of specialized agents (planning, code, testing, review, documentation) that collaborate across an ongoing workflow — each with a defined role, scope, own context/toolset, and protocols for communicating and handing off work.

Use agent teams when the project is long-running and spans many sessions, different aspects genuinely need different specializations, you want agents to build focused expertise without out-of-scope distraction, and you're building something resembling a software-development pipeline.

Persistent role-scoped agents keep context clean and relevant: because each agent focuses only on its domain, a testing agent isn't cluttered with documentation concerns and a code agent isn't distracted by planning — context isolation is the core reason agent teams sustain complex work.

Headless is the fifth pattern (named as one of the five core workflows for non-interactive/programmatic runs) though the article body is truncated before detailing it — relevant to unattended long-running orchestration.
